title: Moroccan carrot salad
subtitle: Warm spiced carrots with cumin and coriander
excerpt: Carrots cooked until just tender, dressed while warm with cumin, lemon, and coriander. The dish served at almost every Moroccan table alongside the main event. Serves four as a side.
sourceNotes: Spiced carrot salad is one of the standard salads of the Moroccan meze table, served as part of a spread of small dishes before the main course. Documented by Paula Wolfert in Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco (1973) and by Claudia Roden in A New Book of Middle Eastern Food (1968). The combination of cumin, garlic, lemon, and coriander is consistent across Moroccan and Tunisian versions.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Keeps refrigerated for three days. Tastes better the next day once the carrots have absorbed the dressing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made an hour ahead and served at room temperature.

content.text: The dressing goes on while the carrots are still warm, which lets them absorb the spices properly. Do not dress cold carrots — they sit on top of the vegetable rather than into it. The dish is served at room temperature rather than hot.

Cook {{carrot}} sliced carrots in boiling salted water for 8–10 minutes until just tender — not soft. They should still have a little bite. Drain well and spread on a tray for 2 minutes to steam off excess moisture. While the carrots are still hot, whisk together {{garlic}} of grated garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, {{coriander-ground}} of ground coriander, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes, the lemon juice, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil, {{honey}} of honey, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Pour the dressing over the warm carrots and toss well to coat. Leave to cool to room temperature. Stir through the coriander just before serving.

items.fix: The dressing must go on while the carrots are still warm and steaming. If they cooled before dressing, warm gently in a pan for 2 minutes before adding the dressing.
Add a little more honey. The balance of acid and sweetness is the point of the dish; adjust to taste.

content.text: Spiced carrot salad appears at the beginning of every proper Moroccan meal alongside an array of other small salads — roasted pepper, preserved lemon, cucumber with herbs. These opening salads are not starters in the French sense; they are part of the fabric of the meal, eaten with bread throughout, replenished as the main courses arrive. The carrot version is the most common and the simplest: the sweetness of cooked carrot with cumin and lemon is one of those combinations that works across cuisines and keeps working.
